title: Store your name in a variable
instruction:
In Chapter 1, you printed your name by typing it directly inside Println. That works, but it's a one-time thing. A variable lets you store your name once and use it anywhere.

Here's how you create a variable in Go:

    name := "Alex"

The := means create this variable and put this value in it. Go figures out the type on its own.

Create a variable called name with your actual name in it, then print it using fmt.Println.
hint: Write name := "YourName" on one line, then fmt.Println(name) on the next. Make sure your name is inside double quotes.

codeChecks.message: Create a variable using :=  like this: name := "YourName"
Print the variable by writing: fmt.Println(name)
Replace the TODO comments with your actual code.

title: Now add your age
instruction:
You're flying! You just created your first variable.

Now let's add a second one. Your age is a number — not text — so it does NOT go in quotes. Go will see the number and automatically know it's an integer.

    age := 24

The name variable is already there. Add an age variable with your actual age, then print both — name first, then age.
hint: age := 24 goes below the name line. Then add a second fmt.Println(age) below the one for name. No quotes around the number!

codeChecks.message: Create an age variable with a number — like: age := 24. No quotes around the number.
Replace the TODO comments with your actual code.

title: Prices need decimals — meet float64
instruction:
Your favorite coffee costs $4.99. Can you store that in an int? Nope — integers are whole numbers only. 4.99 has a decimal point, so you need a float64.

Go figures it out automatically. The moment it sees a decimal point in your number, it treats it as a float64.

    price := 4.99

Add a price variable set to 4.99 and print all three variables: name, age, and price.
hint: price := 4.99 — no quotes, just the number with the decimal. Then add fmt.Println(price) after the other two Println calls.

codeChecks.message: Create a price variable with a decimal number — like: price := 4.99
Replace the TODO comments with your actual code.

title: Yes or no — the bool
instruction:
Last data type for today, and honestly the most dramatic one. A bool can only ever be two things: true or false. That's the whole type. No maybes.

Are you a morning person? If yes: isMorningPerson := true. If no: isMorningPerson := false. Your call — nobody's judging.

Add a variable called isMorningPerson and set it to either true or false, then print all four variables.
hint: isMorningPerson := true (or false if you're a night owl). Then fmt.Println(isMorningPerson). No quotes around true or false — they're special keywords, not text.

codeChecks.message: Create isMorningPerson and set it to true or false — no quotes.
Replace the TODO comments with your actual code.

title: Change your name to a nickname
instruction:
Here's something cool. Variables can change. That's literally what the word variable means — something that varies.

You update a variable using just = (single equals, not :=). The := creates it the first time. The = changes it afterwards.

The program already creates name and prints it. Update name to your nickname after the first print, then print it again. You should see your real name first, then your nickname.
hint: After the first fmt.Println(name), write: name = "Nickname" — single = not := because the variable already exists. Then add another fmt.Println(name).

codeChecks.message: Update name using a single = sign: name = "Nickname"  — not :=
Replace the TODO comments with your actual code.

title: Your complete profile
instruction:
Alright, let's put everything together. You've now met all four data types:

- string  — text, in double quotes
- int     — whole numbers, no quotes
- float64 — decimal numbers, no quotes
- bool    — true or false, no quotes

Create all four from scratch with your own values and print each one. Here's what the output should roughly look like:

    Jordan
    27
    3.75
    true

Use your own name, age, a GPA (make one up if you want), and whether you're a student.
hint: You need four := lines and four Println calls. One for each type. Check the reading material if you need a reminder on the syntax.

codeChecks.message: Create a string variable — it should have double quotes around the value.
Create a float64 variable — it should be a number with a decimal point like 3.75.
Create a bool variable — set it to true or false (no quotes).
Replace all the TODO comments with your actual code.
